name: Cecil Taylor
abstract: Cecil Percival Taylor (March 25, 1929 – April 5, 2018) was an American pianist and poet. Taylor was classically trained and was one of the pioneers of free jazz.

concerts.label: Mary Lou Williams and Cecil Taylor, Piano

works.t: Ziegfeld Follies of 1936: I Can't Get Started
Basic Chords (Bop Changes on the Blues)

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Newport Jazz Festival: Ornette Coleman and Prime Time/ Cecil Taylor Unit
Kool Jazz Festival: Oscar Peterson and Cecil Taylor

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Griffin
about_quotes.evidence: when I was with Sun Ra and Archie Shepp and Cecil Taylor. All these people, when I was playing with those people I played that style
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Griffin interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, February 12, 2024

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Clark
about_quotes.evidence: Cecil Taylor and I did one of the first dates that the – years before the term “avant garde” was coined
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Rashied Ali and Henry Grimes
about_quotes.evidence: I listened to people like Cecil and Henry Grimes and all those guys who were stretching out that stuff... And then you start going that way instead of this way. As long as you keep your mind open, like John and Cecil Taylor and all those cats, they keep their mind open.
about_quotes.doc_title: Rashied Ali and Henry Grimes interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, January 30, 2009

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: For instance, at [the University of] Wisconsin they hired Cecil Taylor for a year. That was a traumatic experience, because I think they said they had something like 1,000 students in the class, and he gave all “F”s, because he didn’t think they were serious.
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Slide Hampton
about_quotes.evidence: Cecil Taylor is mentioned by the interviewer as an example of an American artist grounded in tradition, but Slide Hampton does not mention Cecil Taylor in his response, nor does he describe any personal interaction or relationship with him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Slide Hampton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Roswell Rudd
about_quotes.evidence: The October Revolution in Jazz was the idea of Cecil Taylor and Bill Dixon to get a lot of us together... And we were together long enough to initiate a series of concerts... I will never forget
about_quotes.doc_title: Roswell Rudd interviewed by Monk Rowe, Ellenville, New York, June 23, 1998
